_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 2-12 Head Injury Moderate and Severe TBI (Revised 03/31/2025, MDHHS Reviewed 2025). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan Trauma and Environmental Protocol Section 2-12: Head Injury Moderate and Severe TBI — MDHHS Revised 3/31/25, Reviewed 2025
purpose: Reduce morbidity and mortality in moderate and severe TBI by preventing secondary injury from hypoxia, hyperventilation, hypotension, and uncontrolled hemorrhage.

sections.title: General TBI Management
points: Use this protocol for physical trauma with mechanism for brain injury plus loss of consciousness, altered mental status or GCS < 15, seizure before or after trauma, multisystem trauma requiring positive-pressure ventilation, or decreased level of consciousness/responsiveness in infants
Follow General Trauma-Treatment Protocol and assess hemorrhage using the Bleeding Control protocol
Place the patient on continuous oxygen saturation monitoring and reassess blood pressure every 3–5 minutes
Check blood glucose and treat hypoglycemia when indicated
Consider TXA per Hemorrhagic Shock protocol and contact Medical Control for suspected hemorrhage that does not otherwise meet TXA criteria

sections.title: Airway and Ventilation Management
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 2-12: TBI — Airway and Ventilation
points: All moderate and severe head injury patients receive continuous high-flow oxygen immediately by non-rebreather mask regardless of saturation; maintain SpO2 at or above 90%
Avoid positive-pressure ventilation whenever possible
If hypoxia persists despite high-flow oxygen, reposition the airway and reevaluate
If airway repositioning does not restore SpO2 to at least 90%, or ventilatory effort is inadequate, use two-person BVM with oxygen and basic airway adjuncts
Use advanced airway only when BVM is ineffective or another condition warrants it, such as prolonged transport
If ventilating, maintain normal ventilation and avoid hypo- or hyperventilation; use PCBs/VRTs when available
Ventilation targets: Adults older than 14 years: 10/min; children older than 2 and younger than 14 years: 20/min; infants younger than 2 years: 25/min
Use continuous quantitative ETCO2 when positive-pressure ventilation is used; ideal ETCO2 range is 35–45 mmHg, target 40 mmHg

sections.title: Blood Pressure Management
points: Obtain vascular access in all moderate/severe TBI patients; use IO for hypotension or shock if IV access cannot be quickly established
Do not wait for hypotension: decreasing systolic blood pressure or compensated shock signs require proactive fluid administration

Minimum systolic BP targets: Adults over 14 years: greater than 110 mmHg; pediatrics 11–14 years: greater than 110 mmHg; pediatrics older than 1 and younger than 10 years: greater than 70 + (age x 2) mmHg; 1–12 months: greater than 70 mmHg; younger than 1 month: greater than 60 mmHg
Use NS or LR: Adults receive up to 1 L wide open; pediatrics receive 20 mL/kg
Stop fluids once the BP target is achieved; subsequent boluses may be required if BP drops again

sections.title: Transport Considerations
points: Transport per MCA Transport Protocol to an appropriate trauma destination
Do NOT delay transport for interventions that can be performed en route
Pre-notify receiving facility: mechanism, GCS/mental status, oxygenation, ventilation status, ETCO2 if ventilated, blood pressure trends, hemorrhage control, and fluids given

key_drugs.name: Normal Saline or LR IV/IO (TBI Hypotension)
key_drugs.dose: Adults: up to 1 L wide open; Pediatrics: 20 mL/kg; stop once age-based BP target is achieved
key_drugs.indication: Moderate/severe TBI with hypotension, decreasing systolic BP, or compensated shock signs
key_drugs.contraindications: Avoid hypotonic solutions; stop bolus once target systolic BP is achieved
key_drugs.side_effects: Cerebral edema with excessive fluid; pulmonary edema
key_drugs.notes: Use the 2025 Michigan TBI systolic BP targets, which are higher than older general trauma hypotension thresholds.

deterioration_flags: SpO2 below 90% despite high-flow oxygen — reposition airway; if unresolved, begin two-person BVM with oxygen and airway adjuncts
Positive-pressure ventilation being used without ETCO2 monitoring — add continuous quantitative ETCO2 when available
ETCO2 outside 35–45 mmHg during ventilation — adjust ventilation to avoid hypo- or hyperventilation
Systolic BP below age target or trending down — obtain vascular access and administer NS or LR bolus
Uncontrolled hemorrhage or suspected compensated shock — apply Bleeding Control protocol and consider Hemorrhagic Shock/TXA pathway
scope_notes: GCS assessment, high-flow oxygen by non-rebreather, airway repositioning, and basic airway adjuncts: all levels
Blood glucose assessment: all levels (per MCA selection for MFR)
Two-person BVM ventilation and ventilation rate targets: per scope and agency practice
ETCO2 monitoring, advanced airway, IV/IO access, and fluid bolus: per scope and available equipment
